key_insight: A coupled thermo-mechanical damage model with plasticity and particle methods extends graphite runaway-electron impact simulations from failure onset to post-fracture fragmentation and debris expulsion.
engineering_relevance: The framework improves predictive modeling of plasma-facing material damage under runaway electron loads and supports future assessment of tungsten and other divertor materials.
method: Added Johnson-Holmquist plasticity to nonlinear thermomechanical graphite modeling.
Used an effective plastic-strain failure criterion instead of only strength-based onset criteria.
Coupled finite element analysis with smoothed-particle hydrodynamics to capture fragmentation and debris ejection.
evidence_signal: The extended model was benchmarked against two controlled runaway-electron damage experiments performed on DIII-D.
watch_item: The approach is validated on graphite experiments, so transfer to tungsten or other materials will require careful recalibration and further benchmarking.
paper_title: Runaway electron induced explosions of graphite; modeling versus controlled DIII-D experiments
